title: Flask Templates – Dynamic HTML with Jinja2

meta.title: Flask Templates Guide – Jinja2, Inheritance & Control Flow (2026)
meta.description: Learn how to use Jinja2 templates in Flask to build dynamic web pages. Master template inheritance, variables, loops, and conditional logic.

content.value: While APIs return JSON, web applications often need to serve HTML. Flask uses the **Jinja2** engine to generate dynamic HTML. This allows you to write standard HTML files with special placeholders and logic that Flask fills in before sending the page to the user's browser.
By default, Flask looks for HTML files in a folder named `templates/` at the root of your project. You use the `render_template()` function to serve these files and pass data to them.
Jinja2 uses specific delimiters to distinguish between plain HTML and Python-like logic.

value: **{{ variable }}**: Expressions used to print output to the page.
**{% statement %}**: Control structures like loops or conditionals.
**{# comment #}**: Comments that won't appear in the final HTML.

content.value:
Welcome, {{ name }}!

Your Skills:

    {% for skill in tech_stack %}
  • {{ skill }}
    • {% else %}
  • No skills listed.
    • {% endfor %}
To avoid repeating headers, footers, and navigation bars on every page, Flask uses inheritance. You create a base 'layout' and other pages 'extend' it.

Navigation Bar
{% block content %}{% endblock %} {% extends 'base.html' %} {% block content %}

This is the Home Page

{% endblock %}

rows: Imports a small snippet of HTML

faq.question: Can I use Python functions inside Jinja2?
faq.answer: Not directly. You can only use functions that you explicitly pass to render_template or those that Flask provides by default (like url_for).

faq.question: What is the 'safe' filter?
faq.answer: By default, Jinja2 escapes HTML for security. If you want to render actual HTML tags stored in a variable, use {{ my_html|safe }}. Be careful with user input!

faq.question: Where do I put my CSS and Images?
faq.answer: Put them in a folder named 'static/'. In your template, reference them using: {{ url_for('static', filename='css/main.css') }}.
